technical_quality: Diagnostic-quality photo of a paper ECG with mild baseline wander/perspective distortion; all leads are interpretable. Standard calibration appears to be 25 mm/s and 10 mm/mV.
rhythm: Sinus rhythm with frequent monomorphic premature ventricular complexes, predominantly in a ventricular trigeminy pattern.
p_wave: Sinus P waves are present before conducted beats and are upright in the inferior leads; no definite right or left atrial enlargement is identified.
qrs_morphology: Conducted QRS complexes are narrow with rightward frontal axis. Frequent unifocal PVCs are present. R-wave progression is acceptable across the precordium. No definite bundle branch block, no convincing pathologic Q waves, no clear LVH/RVH by voltage, and no delta waves/pre-excitation.
st_segments: Mild diffuse/downsloping ST-segment depression on sinus beats, most evident in inferolateral and anterolateral leads; no focal ST-segment elevation or convincing reciprocal STEMI pattern.
t_waves: T waves are flattened to mildly inverted on sinus beats, particularly in the anterior/lateral and inferior leads; no hyperacute or peaked T waves.
u_waves: Prominent positive U waves are present, especially in V2-V6 and also visible in the inferior leads, with apparent QU prolongation.
specific_patterns: No STEMI pattern (no focal ST elevation or convincing reciprocal acute infarct pattern)
No atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, or regular SVT
No bundle branch block; possible rightward fascicular conduction abnormality/LPFB is not definitive
Hypokalemia pattern: ST depression, T-wave flattening/inversion, prominent U waves, and ventricular ectopy
final_interpretation: Sinus rhythm with frequent monomorphic PVCs and right axis deviation. The combination of diffuse ST depression, T-wave flattening/inversion, and prominent U waves is most consistent with hypokalemia/electrolyte disturbance causing ventricular ectopy; no STEMI is seen.
differential: Hypokalemia (most likely): prominent U waves, T-wave flattening, ST depression, and frequent ventricular ectopy strongly support this
Digoxin effect with or without concomitant hypokalemia: can cause scooped ST depression and ventricular ectopy if clinically relevant
Subendocardial ischemia/NSTEMI: diffuse ST depression can be ischemic, but the marked U waves and overall pattern favor electrolyte disturbance over acute coronary occlusion
caveats: Single photographed tracing without a long rhythm strip; frequent PVCs and prominent U waves make rate and QT/QTc estimation approximate. Axis assessment is based on photographed limb leads and could be affected by lead placement or image distortion.
